questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with recurrent renal colic. Serum calcium is 2.85 mmol/L and PTH is 12 pmol/L (elevated). She also has galactorrhea and irregular menses. Her father died of a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of hyperparathyroidism, prolactinoma (galactorrhea), and family history of pancreatic NET is classic for MEN1. Sporadic primary hyperparathyroidism is common but lacks other features; FHH has low calcium/creatinine clearance ratio.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man is found to have a thyroid nodule. Fine-needle aspiration shows medullary thyroid carcinoma. He has a history of poorly controlled hypertension with episodic palpitations and sweating. His 24-hour urinary metanephrines are elevated. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Coexisting medullary thyroid carcinoma and pheochromocytoma strongly suggests MEN2A. MEN2B would have additional mucosal neuromas and marfanoid habitus; NF1 can have pheochromocytoma but not MTC.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with intestinal obstruction due to colonic ganglioneuromatosis on biopsy. He has a marfanoid appearance with long limbs and a high-arched palate. He also reports palpitations and sweating. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Ganglioneuromatosis, marfanoid habitus, and possible pheochromocytoma (palpitations/sweating) are classic for MEN2B. MEN2A lacks intestinal neuromas; Marfan syndrome does not include neuroendocrine tumors.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with severe epigastric pain and melena. Upper GI endoscopy shows multiple duodenal ulcers. Fasting gastrin level is 850 pg/mL (elevated). He has a history of recurrent kidney stones and his serum calcium is 2.80 mmol/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hypercalcemia from hyperparathyroidism (kidney stones) together with Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma) is typical for MEN1. Sporadic gastrinoma is less likely to have concurrent hyperparathyroidism.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with known MEN1 has undergone total parathyroidectomy for hyperparathyroidism and is currently asymptomatic. On annual surveillance, imaging shows a small non-functioning pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (1 cm). She has no symptoms. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In known MEN1, a small non-functioning pancreatic NET is typical of the syndrome. Sporadic NET is less likely given the background. MEN2A rarely involves pancreatic NET; the tumor is non-functioning, so not insulinoma or glucagonoma.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with recurrent renal stones. Serum calcium is 2.75 mmol/L (corrected, normal 2.2–2.6) and PTH is 12 pmol/L (normal 1.6–6.9). Her father had a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumour. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of MEN1?
questions.explanation: The combination of primary hyperparathyroidism with a family history of an MEN1-associated tumour (pancreatic NET) strongly suggests MEN1. Genetic testing for MEN1 mutation confirms the diagnosis. CT abdomen is not diagnostic for MEN1; prolactin and glucose screen for other MEN1 components but do not confirm the syndrome; 24h urinary calcium helps differentiate familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia, not confirm MEN1.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known MEN2A (RET mutation) attends annual surveillance. He is asymptomatic, but plasma free metanephrines are mildly elevated (2 times upper normal). Which test is most appropriate to confirm the presence of a pheochromocytoma?
questions.explanation: In a patient with MEN2 and elevated plasma metanephrines, the next step is imaging (CT or MRI) to localize the tumour. Repeat testing or 24h collection adds little; MIBG is reserved for equivocal cases; clonidine test is not first-line. CT abdomen confirms anatomical presence of adrenal mass.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with known MEN1 presents to the emergency department after a episode of confusion and sweating. Capillary glucose is 2.2 mmol/L. Which single test is most appropriate to confirm that her hypoglycaemia is due to an insulinoma?
options: Serum insulin and C-peptide during hypoglycaemia
questions.explanation: In a patient with documented hypoglycaemia, measuring insulin and C-peptide levels at the time of hypoglycaemia demonstrates inappropriate insulin secretion. A 72h fast is used if hypoglycaemia is not captured; OGTT is not diagnostic; HbA1c reflects overall control; CT finds tumour but does not confirm diagnosis.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old man presents with a neck mass and diarrhoea. He has a marfanoid habitus and multiple mucosal neuromas on his lips and tongue. Serum calcitonin is 450 pg/mL (normal <10). Which test is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of MEN2B?
options: Fine needle aspiration of the thyroid nodule
questions.explanation: The clinical features (marfanoid, neuromas) plus elevated calcitonin strongly suggest medullary thyroid carcinoma due to MEN2B. Genetic testing for RET mutation (specifically M918T) confirms the syndrome. Ultrasound and FNA identify MTC but not the syndrome; pentagastrin is rarely available and not necessary; calcium is for hyperparathyroidism screening.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with known MEN1 and treated hyperparathyroidism presents with severe epigastric pain and watery diarrhoea. Fasting gastrin is 800 pg/mL (normal <100). Which investigation best confirms the presence of a gastrinoma?
questions.explanation: In patients with hypergastrinaemia, a secretin stimulation test (paradoxical increase in gastrin >200 pg/mL) is specific for gastrinoma and distinguishes it from other causes such as antral G-cell hyperplasia or retained gastric antrum. Endoscopy may show ulcers but does not confirm gastrinoma; CT and Octreoscan localise but do not confirm the diagnosis; gastric acid analysis measures acid output but is less specific.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known MEN1 presents with epigastric pain and diarrhoea. Upper GI endoscopy reveals multiple duodenal ulcers. Serum gastrin is markedly elevated. Which is the most appropriate first-line definitive therapy for his gastrinoma?
options: Proton pump inhibitor (e.g., omeprazole 40 mg daily)
questions.explanation: In MEN1-associated gastrinoma, PPIs are first-line to control acid hypersecretion; surgery is not curative due to multifocality and high recurrence. Octreotide is reserved for metastatic disease, and chemotherapy is for advanced cases.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with a known RET mutation (MEN2A) undergoes screening and is found to have an elevated calcitonin level with a 5 mm medullary thyroid carcinoma on ultrasound. She is asymptomatic. Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection
questions.explanation: Total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection is the definitive treatment for MTC in MEN2, as MTC is resistant to radioiodine and external beam radiation. TKIs are reserved for metastatic disease. Observation is not appropriate given the RET mutation and elevated calcitonin.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with MEN2B presents with a palpable thyroid nodule and hypertension. Urinary metanephrines are elevated. CT reveals a 4 cm left adrenal mass and a 2 cm thyroid nodule. Which surgical procedure should be performed first?
questions.explanation: Pheochromocytoma must be resected first (with α- and β-blockade) to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis during thyroidectomy. Adrenalectomy is prioritised over thyroidectomy in MEN2B when both MTC and pheo are present.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with known MEN1 presents to the emergency department with confusion. Serum calcium is 3.5 mmol/L (normal 2.2–2.6), PTH is elevated. After initial resuscitation with intravenous fluids, which is the most appropriate definitive therapy for her hyperparathyroid crisis?
questions.explanation: Parathyroidectomy is the definitive treatment for hyperparathyroid crisis in MEN1 to rapidly lower calcium and prevent recurrence. Medical therapy (bisphosphonates, calcitonin, cinacalcet) is used as a bridge or if surgery is contraindicated, but not definitive.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known MEN2A and a history of bilateral pheochromocytoma resected 3 years ago presents with palpitations and hypertension. Her urine metanephrines are elevated, and a new 3 cm adrenal mass is found. Before planned surgery, which drug is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Non-selective β-blockers like propranolol are contraindicated in pheochromocytoma without prior α-blockade, as unopposed α-mediated vasoconstriction can precipitate hypertensive crisis. Labetalol has both α and β blocking properties and is safe; phenoxybenzamine and doxazosin are α-blockers used preoperatively.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a history of recurrent renal stones. Serum calcium is 2.75 mmol/L (albumin-adjusted). He is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange a CT scan of the neck
questions.explanation: The first step in investigating hypercalcaemia is to confirm elevated PTH to differentiate primary hyperparathyroidism from other causes. CT neck is appropriate only after biochemical confirmation; calcium supplementation is wrong; surgery is not first; 24h urine calcium is adjunct but PTH is key.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with known MEN1 syndrome and gastrinoma is on omeprazole 40 mg once daily. She reports recurrent epigastric pain and diarrhoea. Her heartburn has not improved. There is no weight loss. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Increase omeprazole to 80 mg once daily
questions.explanation: In gastrinoma, the first step for symptomatic control is to increase PPI dose, as breakthrough symptoms indicate insufficient acid suppression. Checking gastrin may be done but is not the immediate therapeutic action; surgery is not first-line; octreotide scan is for localization.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with known MEN2A and a phaeochromocytoma (size 4 cm) presents with sudden onset of severe headache, palpitations, and sweating. Blood pressure is 220/115 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm. He has not started any alpha-blockade. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: The priority in phaeochromocytoma crisis is alpha-blockade to control hypertension and prevent hypertensive crisis; beta-blockade (labetalol) without prior alpha-blockade can cause unopposed alpha stimulation. CT and metanephrines are not immediate; nitroglycerin is not specific.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with MEN1 and primary hyperparathyroidism had a parathyroidectomy 2 days ago. He now becomes confused. Serum calcium (corrected) is 3.25 mmol/L. ECG shows short QT interval. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Hypercalcaemic crisis first requires aggressive IV rehydration to improve renal calcium excretion. Calcitonin or bisphosphonates are subsequent steps; checking PTH is not immediate; renal ultrasound not urgent.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is found to have a RET proto-oncogene mutation during family screening. She has no symptoms. Her serum calcium and calcitonin are normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start surveillance with annual serum calcitonin
Commence oral calcium and vitamin D
questions.explanation: In MEN2 carriers, prophylactic thyroidectomy is considered based on calcitonin levels and mutation risk; but immediate step is surveillance with calcitonin to detect early MTC. Referral to genetics may have already been done; calcium/vitamin D not needed; ultrasound is adjunct but not first surveillance.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known MEN1 (previous parathyroidectomy, gastrinoma) presents to the emergency department with severe epigastric pain and haematemesis. She is haemodynamically unstable. Which finding is the most immediate red flag for a life-threatening complication?
options: Erect chest X-ray showing free gas under the diaphragm
Hb drop of 20 g/L over 6 hours
CT abdomen showing thickened gastric folds
questions.explanation: Free gas under the diaphragm indicates a perforated viscus, a life-threatening complication of Zollinger-Ellison syndrome from gastrinoma. This is an immediate red flag for surgical emergency, whereas hypercalcaemia or elevated gastrin are expected in MEN1 but not acutely dangerous here.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with no known medical history presents with sudden severe headache, vomiting, and visual disturbance. Examination reveals bitemporal hemianopia and ptosis. Which finding confirms the most life-threatening diagnosis that must be excluded?
options: MRI head showing pituitary macroadenoma with haemorrhage
questions.explanation: Pituitary apoplexy (haemorrhage into a pituitary adenoma) is a life-threatening emergency that can occur in MEN1. MRI showing haemorrhage confirms the diagnosis. Prolactinoma is common but not immediately dangerous; apoplexy requires urgent neurosurgery.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with a family history of MEN2A presents with palpitations, sweating, and severe hypertension (220/130 mmHg). She is found to have a thyroid nodule. Which investigation result is a red flag for an immediately life-threatening condition?
options: Thyroid ultrasound showing a 2 cm solid nodule
questions.explanation: Markedly elevated urinary catecholamines indicate phaeochromocytoma, which can cause hypertensive crisis, stroke, or myocardial infarction. This is an immediate red flag requiring alpha-blockade before any surgery. Calcitonin elevation suggests medullary thyroid cancer but is less acutely life-threatening.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with MEN1 and known hyperparathyroidism (calcium 2.85 mmol/L) attends a routine clinic. He reports feeling ‘more tired than usual’ and has mild confusion. Which finding is the strongest red flag for severe hypercalcaemia requiring admission?
questions.explanation: Serum calcium above 3.0 mmol/L is a red flag for hypercalcaemic crisis, which can cause dehydration, renal failure, and cardiac arrest. The patient's confusion and tiredness suggest symptomatic hypercalcaemia. PTH elevation is expected; QTc interval is usually shortened, not prolonged.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man presents with abdominal distension, vomiting, and constipation for 3 days. He has a marfanoid habitus, multiple mucosal neuromas on his lips and tongue, and a thyroid nodule. Which diagnosis is the most immediate life-threatening mimic of an acute abdomen?
questions.explanation: Intestinal ganglioneuromatosis is a feature of MEN2B that can cause bowel obstruction or perforation, presenting as an acute abdomen. This must be considered before other complications. Phaeochromocytoma could cause hypertensive crises but not obstruction. Medullary thyroid cancer is typically indolent.
